This fire was reported days ago by a park visitor about smoke on the Alta Peak Trail. ECC-1, DV-3 and Fuels 3 hiked the area and found no sign of smoke or fire. A few days later, a new report came in from one of the Giant Forest interpretive rangers who said there was smoke on the Alta trail towards Panther Gap. She described the smoke as being just off the trail a good distance before Panther Gap, burning about a tenth of acre in some low brush. She stated there were some flames but that they weren't high and some visitors had been throwing dirt on the fire. SEKI Engine 51 Module was dispatched to the scene where direct suppression was performed due to the fire's proximity to established trails. After two weeks in patrol status, the fire was officially declared out by park duty officer DV-4 on 7/24/16 at 1211.

The Atwell Fire was ignited by lightning and was discovered on 7-14-02 by a    squad of Crew 91.  The fire occurred in an area which was burned in a 1995     prescribed fire.  Fire activity was minimal with creeping, smouldering ground  fire, and the occassional torching of trees or brush.  The fire was checked    every other day by fire monitors for about 8 days.  Because the fire was       located in a prescribed burn unit scheduled for ignition in 2002, Crew 7 from  Yosemite was ordered to rescrape a handline along Paradise Ridge as a          contingency.  If the fire had become active, this line would have been used to contain the fire south of the ridge.  This holding action is what drove up the cost per acre to the final level.  As it turned out there was not enough       continuous fuels to sustain fire spread and the fire was called out on 8-4-02.

A lightning storm ignited the Lost Canyon fire. It was discovered by trail crew personnel on July 6th, 2007 and called into fire dispatch. Two monitors were dispatched to assess the situation and were flown into the site by H552. The .5 acre fire was smoldering though the perimeter was inactive. They spent 1 day examining nearby fuels and adjacent trail systems, gave the fire a low potential for growth status, so we decided to occasionally monitor the fire by air as needed. The fire was located due west of Moraine Lake where Lost Canyon spills into the Big Arroyo.  Based upon its remote location, it was decided to use the fire for resource benefit. The fire slowly smoldered until it was declared out on 7/29/07 at .5 acres by Dave Allen.

On July 13, 2002 park helicoopter, H552, reported smoke in the Slide Bluffs    area not far from Lake of the Fallen Moon.  Closer investigation determined    that lightning had ignited a small fire on the upper third of the slope. The   fire was burning in high altitude Red Fir Fuels(SEKI custom Fule Model 18) and was predominately contained by rocky slopes.  Intial size-up determined that   fire behavior was generally smoldering to creeping with very low rates of      spread.  There was little potential for the fire to spread much beyond 0.25    acre.  Consequently, it was determined that the fire could best be managed be  managed for resource benefits.                                                 The fire was monitor by air and continued to show smoke for the next several   weeks, altough little ground spread was evident. The last smoke from the fire  was seen in mid-August.  The fire placed inactive  status and declared out on  November 11, 2002 after several inches of rain and snowfall.

Lightning ignited a small fire in the Muir Grove area of Sequoia Ntl Park.  The fire was detected by one of our fire personnel and called into dispatch on 9/10/08.  The following day, eight members of the Arrowhead hotshots were dispatched to the fire to put it out due to its proximity to the boundary line with the USFS.  After the initial attack phase of the fire was completed, Crew 91 took over the mop-up and patrol until the fire was declared out on 9/18/08 by John Ziegler.

On 8/7/06, Fuels Assistant Uhr spotted a new fire start while doing a standard park recon, in H552, following a period of high lightning activity.  The Ridge fire was burning due west of another fire use fire known as the Roaring fire.  Both were in close proximity to Sentinel Dome in Kings Canyon Ntl. Park.  Upon discovery, the fire was .1 acres burning on a NW aspect in fuel model 10.  Though closer to the USFS/NPS boundary line than the Roaring fire, it was decided to use this fire for resource benefit as well, with the knowledge that it was quite possible that the two fires would eventually merge into one larger fire.  The fire grew slowlly with predominantly backing and flanking actions.  It was monitored by H552, who did aerial recons of the fire, along with several fire monitors.  On 9/25/06 the fire had grown to 139 acres and Duty Officer Allen confirmed that it had indeed joined the Roaring fire on that date.  From that point forward we treated the fire as one unit and added all of the additional acreage to the Roaring fire use fire.  The fire burned without incident until it was declared out on 12/29/06 by FMO Dave Bartlett after a lengthy period of inactivity and several rain/snow storms.

This human caused fire was reported by park visitors Toby Walker and Michael Supper in the Redwood Meadow area to Ranger 812. It was described as being just after Cliff Creek Crossing on the Paradise Ridge Trail in the sequoia grove. It was said to have been an out of bounds campfire with unknown spread potential but moderate at least. The individuals stated they tried to put it out but it began to smoke again as they ate lunch. They took photos of the area and provided them to the ranger. H-552 did a recon of the area and inserted three helitack personnel. Charles Mathias was IC and Boris Botchev and Eric Campbell were FFT2. Their plan was to put a quick line around the fire to confine it and then hike to the landing zone for extraction. The IC stated no suppression was needed. Upon completion of the scratch line, the IC stated the fire was completely out. No known suspects have been identified at this time.

On 6/08/06 at 10:00 hrs., Ranger 4116 called in a smoke report to District Ranger Kelleher.  Kelleher then called fire dispatch and let us know that a lightning caused fire had been sighted in the Arrow Creek drainage, due east of Parardise Valley.  While flying the area, H552 located a second smoke which turned out to be the Woods fire.  This .1 acre fire, CA-KNP-0011, was burning on a southeast aspect in western white pine, on an 80% slope.  Spread was likely due to the steepness of the terrain and the continuity of the fuel bed.  The fire, however, was also very remotely located between Arrow and Woods Creeks, roughly a half a mile from the original smoke report.  Due to its remote location, FMO Bartlett made the decision to use the fire for resource benefit.  The fire was subsequently monitored by aircraft until it was declared out on 7/13/06.

The Baldy West fire was started by lightning on August 31st.  It was discovered jointly by the Buck Rock fire lookout and an air attack ship that was scanning for new starts.  Located just west of Big Baldy, near the USFS boundary line and a major tourist thoroughfare, it was determined that we needed to suppress this fire.  Initially DFMO Rosso took IC of the fire while Sequoia Ntl. Forest resources were enroute.  Cobra 5 and P33(the one fire fighter mentioned above) initial attacked the fire.  Once the crews were on scene and had increased their situational awareness, Mike Carrol (from SQF Cobra 5) took over the fire.  The crews worked it through the first shift in conjunction w/ H-552 who dropped 4 buckets of water in order to cool it down.  The next day Cobra 5 returned w/ith SQF E-32.  They continued to mop up the fire and at 1500 hourse, Carrol called it contained.  The fire was then placed in patrol status.  Over the next few weeks various resources checked the fire to ensure that it was indeed out.  On 9/24, it was officially declared out by DFMO Rosso.

On 7/24/06 H552 discovered the Trail fire while doing a standard park recon after a period of severe fire weather and high lightning activity. This .1 acre fire, burning in fuel model 8, near Paradise Peak in Sequoia Ntl. Park was determined to be a suppression fire by duty officer Allen. Due to the high volume of new starts we were experiencing that day, we did not get back out to the fire site until 7/26. At that time H552 who was rechecking all of our new fires, noticed that there was no smoke showing on the fire. On several subsequent checks the same pattern was repeated: no smoke. However on August 9th 2006, an aerial recon discovered that the fire was showing a minimal amount of activity. At this point, we dispatched 2 firefighters : McKibben as ITC5 trainee and Mike White, her trainer. They contianed the fire on Aug. 11th at .25 acres and requested 2 additional firefighters to assist with mop up. Two FEMO's were thus flown out of the fire on 8/11/06 to assist.  The fire was declared out on 9/04/06/2006 by DFMO, Dave Allen.

The Palisades fire was started by lightning on or about July 17th, 2002.       Because of the remoteness of the terrain, the fire wasn't discovered until     8-26-2002.  At the time of discovery, the fire was approximately 70 acres in   size actively buring and torching.  Because of the heavy downed fuel           accumulation, it was likely that the fire could easily exceed 1,000 acres.     Most significant value to be protected was the John Muir Trail, which ran      immediately through the middle of the fire.  The decision was made to manage   the fire for resource benefits.                                                 The fire was initially monitored by air.  However, when it became apparent     that significant runs were imminent, on ground monitoring was used to manage   trail traffic and document burning conditions and fire spread.  Full scale on  the ground monitoring began 8-20 and continued through September 10.  By mid   September, heaviest fuels in the immediate path of the fire had been consumed  and the fire had most of the fire's perimeter had burned into rock.  Minor     potential remained for slow westward movement.                                  The fire continued to smolder and was monitored by air until mid November when it was extinguished by heavy rain and snowfall on 11-11-2002.

This human caused fire was actually reported by the park 24 hour dispatch on 10/25/15 at 0130 AM. It was reported to be next to Quarters 83 in Lodgepole housing. Dispatcher Jane Lobato said that two Lodgepole Law Enforcement rangers were on scene and wanted to know if they should start direct attack. The on-call dispatcher for AMFD called Kings District FMO John Ziegler at home to ask him what he wanted to do about this fire. He got a structure engine to the fire and for initial attack and before the next morning it was 60% contained. SEKI Engine 72 was dispatched to scene the next morning to continue with mop-up and patrol the fire. Per Kings District FMO, John Ziegler, this fire was started accidentally by a Lodgepole resident who dumped the ash from their heating stove onto the ground. This fire was officially declared out by Sequoia District FMO, Robert Sanders on 11/2/15 at 1200.

The Morraine fire was started by lightning on July 13th, 2002 and dicovered by NPS helicopter 552 during a reconnaissance flight onJuly 18th, 2002 at         approximately 1400 hours.  Fire was buring mid-slope in Red Fir fuels.  Rate   of spread was slow and fire behavior was smoldering to creeping. The fire  was declared out after heavy rains on November 11th, 2002.

This fire was reported to the park via a social media site. Hikers in the area posted pictures of the fire on the JMT Facebook page. The fire was described by the reporting parties as smoke between Vidette Meadow and Junction Meadow near Cedar Grove. The Kings District FMO saw the pictures and asked Ash Mountain Fire to order a Type 2 Helicopter and helitack crew from Yosemite National Park. H-551 performed a recon of the area and described the fire as downcanyon from the Vidette Meadow, north of the river. The fire was a quarter of an acre in size, burning in lodgepole pine with low fire behavior. Once on the ground, the helitack crew determined the fire to be human caused as they found a fire ring in the vicinity.  The fire was smoldering and creeping with low spread potential. 5 Helitack crewmembers confined and contained the fire with no additional resources that same day. The next day, 3 Hotshot crewmembers were inserted into the fire and completed 100% mop-up.

This fire of unknown cause was reported by Backcountry Ranger 554 Delta as being located a quarter of a mile east of the Sugarloaf Peak trail and was described as a single tree burning in Manzanita and Pine with six foot flames. H-552 did a recon of the fire area and described it also as being 1/4 mile east of Sugarloaf Peak on the trail burning in timber. The helicopter crew also described seeing six foot flame lengths, actively burning with moderate to heavy spread potential. Four members of the helicopter crew were inserted into the fire to construct a line around the fire and perform MIST tactics to keep the fire within the constructed perimeter. H-552 also performed 5 bucket drops on this fire to aid in control and mop-up. During suppression tactics the ground crew noticed a hoof print and said it looked as if someone had tried to cover up the point of origin. The point of origin was described as a campfire that got out of control and the parties tried to cover it up rather than extinguish. It was further described as "some kind of fire humans tried to put out by putting debris on top of it and tried to cover it up." It looked to the ground crew as if it had been burning the night before it was reported, maybe 8/20/18. An investigation was performed by park Law Enforcement Rangers but there has been no official release of subject identities. This fire was officially declared out by DFMO Kelly Singer on 10/3/18 at 1300.

IT WAS DECIDED TO SUPPRESS THE FIRE THE    FOLLOWING DAY DUE TO THE HEAVY FUEL LOADINGS, TIME OF THE YEAR AND PROXIMITY   TO THE PARK BOUNDARY.  A MEDIUM HELICOPTER AND RAPPELL CREW OF 6 WAS BROUGHT   IN THE DAY AFTER DISCOVERY AND THIS CREW JOINED A PARK CREW OF 5 IN THE        INITIAL ATTACK EFFORT ON 8/9/95.  THE RAPPELL CREW FLEW IN, CUT THE HELISPOT   AND THEN THE PARK CREW WAS FLOWN IN.  INITIAL ATTACK STARTED AT APPROXIMATELY  1800 AND THE LINE WAS COMPLETED BY 0900 ON 8/10/95 AND MOP-UP BEGAN.  THE      SEQUOIA TREE WAS BURNING HIGH UP AND SO THE FIRE WAS NOT DECLARED CONTROLLED   UNTIL 1600 HOURS ON 8/12/95.  THE FIRE WAS NOT DECLARED OUT UNTIL 12/7/95      BECAUSE SEQUOIA TREES HAVE BEEN KNOWN TO SMOLDER FOR MONTHS.

The Woods Creek fire was discovered by Ryan Whiteside, a backpacker passing through the Woods Creek area of Kings Canyon Ntl. Parks.  Upon going through this area on Oct. 9th, he discovered a small human caused warming fire that had been left unattended in the backcountry campsite.  He took some actions to stop the fire by putting water on it and ringing the fire with rocks.  When he left, it was not however out, but smoldering.  He hiked into the Cedar Grove ranger station on Oct. 11th and reported the fire to Ned Kelleher, a Cedar Grove ranger, who then reported the fire to us.  One month has passed with no smoke reports from visitors and/or aircraft and in the interim we have had a good deal of inclement weather, including snow and rain.  Thus the fire was declared out by DFMO Rosso on 11/9/07 at 1800 hours.

On 7/24/06, H552 discovered the Alta fire (CA-KNP-0037) on a standard park recon following severe weather and lightning activity.  The fire, which was burning 1/2 of a mile to the SW of Tharps Rock, near Alta Peak, was .1 acres burning in red fir/mixed conifer forest.  The fire was smoldering on the ground, near the Middle Fork drainage.  Because of its location, it was suppressed due to the sensitive nature of smoke drifting down valley into the neighboring town of Three Rivers, CA.  The fire was quickly lined on 7/24, however part of a C tree fell out and breached the line necessitating additional work on the part of the crew.  The fire was put into patrol status on 7/26 when the crew returned to the Ash Mt. helibase.  9622 Ragusin did an ICT5 trainee assignment on this fire under the tutelage of Ted Young.  Two additional fire fighters assisted in this effort.  The fire was declared out on 8/7/06 after being monitored by air for several weeks.

The following changes were made to this Fire Report as part of the conversion from SACS to WFMI on 05/14/2005. TYPE INIT ATTACK 2: R - Mapped to T End of SACS to WFMI conversion notes.  The fire monitor crew discovered a smoking pile on August 22, while they were  retrieving fule moisture samples for the Sunset D burn, burning in the old     Grant Grove Rock Quarry.  Nobody claimed responsibilty for the pile.  After    calling Fire Dispatch, I (led fire monitor) was authorized to be IC and        requested an engine to be dispatched.  Greg Z(GG) responded with a patrol      from GG and E-72 came form Giant FOrest.  By the time resources arrived the    pile was fully involved and I decided to let the pile burn all the way down.    Crews helped with a little containment line and making sure nothing was going  to roll out after we left.  After watching the pile burn down for a bit I      thought it as now safe to release crews.  The next day the Grant Grove Fire    Personnel checked the pile and confirmed it was out.  After investigation I    found out that the people who dispose of campground ashes had dumped some, not checking if there were any hot embers still in the ash.  The ashes were dumped to close to a pile meant to be burned in the winter.  Rangers were inforrmed   and the ashes will be checked next time.

At that time, ENGB Greg Ver was alerted to the problem as he drove by the welding site.  Using E-72 and his crew for support, he rapidly engaged the fire and quickly put it out.  The fire was caught at .1 acres and was declared out on 5/29/08 by DFMO Allen.

This human caused fire was reported by Ranger 513 as an escaped campfire in Azalea Campground Site #38. 513 said a campfire was started inside a ring and it appeared as though the individual responsible had dragged a big log into the ring and then threw it out of the ring while it was still burning the next morning. It was described as about 4 feet of the underside of the log was burnt and burning. Capt 51 responded and a chainsaw was used to efficiently remove all of the burning/smoldering fire on the log. This was a human caused fire near Pan Point helispot in Grant Grove. It was started by an out of bounds campfire. YOSE E-43 was covering in the park and discovered it while patrolling. The fire was on the Sequoia National Forest District and we touched base with them. They had 2 patrols, one crew and an Air Attack overhead. YOSE E-4 arrived on the ground first and applied a hose lay and began mop up. H-552 was ordered but was stood down minutes later. SEKI E-74 arrived on scene and described the fire as 4x4 in size and an out of bounds campfire near the Pan Point trail and parking area. On 10/25/05 the Buck Rock Lookout reported a nebulous smoke in the Cabin Creek area.  Due to foul weather the smoke was not investigated until 10/26.  At that time, Helicopter 552 went out on an aerial recon of the whole park since we had experienced an enormous lightning bust the day before.  They went armed with the coordinates supplied by the lookout and confirmed that there was indeed a small fire burning in the Cabin Meadow area of Sequoia Ntl. Park.  The .1 acre fire was smoldering and burning with open flames in fuel model 8 at 7,200 ft., on a S. aspect.  Two fire monitors were sent to search for the fire that day but they were unable to locate it.  H552 located it again on 10/27/05 and advised that if monitors were to take a 318 degree bearing from the cabin and proceed approx. 3/4 of a mile they would find this small fire.  Over the next several days the fire area was shrouded in inclement weather, so no further troops were deployed.  Due to its remote location, the lateness of the season, and favorable weather conditions, it was decided that this was an excellent candidate for a wildland fire used for resource benefit.  The fire was monitored by air and ground crews until it was declared out on 12/10/05 at a total of 14.6 acres.  No injuries were incurred.

The Goliath wildfire, CA-KNP-0007, was started by lightning on May 16, 2006 in the Redwood Canyon area of Kings Canyon Ntl. Parks.  Because of the heavy accumulation of fuel in the area, the earliness of the season, and the proximity to values at risk, it was decided that this small fire would be suppressed.  E51 was dispatched to do the initial attack.  Later, various groups of firefighters followed to patrol the area and to mop up.  On May 19th, at 0827 it was called contained by Clint Coonfield.  The fire was occasionally monitored by park resources and on 6/19/06, H552 flew the fire and reported that no smoke was visible.  A hot, dry weather pattern set up over the area and by 6/24/06 ranger 512 called in a smoke report.  H552 was launched and it was determined that the Goliath fire was experiencing renewed fire activity.  A quick size up was done, then 3 members of the helitack crew were dispatched to IA the fire.  The .1 acre slop quickly grew to 2 acres and McKibben, the IC in training, requested bucket drops.  H552 was dispatched with a long line.  It was also determined that additional hand power was needed so on 6/25/06, the Arrowhead hotshots were deployed to assist in digging line.  Arrowhead caught the fire at 12.0 acres and the fire was called controlled on July 3rd.  Arrowhead investigated the fire while on scene and determined that there had possibly been a second strike tree which could have resulted in the rekindle.  Subsequent reviews of the BLM lightning maps did indicate that there had been numerous strikes within the vicinity of the original fire.  Though it will never be known for certain it is quite possible that this was a second fire that just happened to start right near the vicinity of the first Goliath.  Since it could not be determined for sure, we made the decision to treat the fire as one and the same to simplify matters.  On 8/15/06, this fire was declared out by Dave Allen after a lengthy period of inactivity.

On 7/24/06, following a period of severe fire weather and high lightning activity, H552 found the Silliman fire during a standard park recon. This brush fire was reported to Ash Mountain Fire Dispatch by one of the Sequoia Shuttle drivers who received the report from a park visitor. The fire was located 1/4 mile from where the Huckleberry trail meets the main park road. The fire cause was unknown but was burning in fern and ground fuels and a large downed log at the foot of a sequoia tree. Fire behavior was creeping and smoldering with low to moderate spread potential. Direct suppression was performed, due to location, by helitack crewmember 52B and four members of Crew 91. After two weeks in patrol status, this fire was officially declared out by the park fuels technician on 7/23/16 at 2000.

The fire was burning in the fuel model 18 in moderate to steep terrain.  The   fire was burning actively with low rates of spread due to rock bounds.  The    cause of the fire was lightning. Fire was reported by a back country ranger and due to the low values at risk and high elevation determination was made to manage fire under monitoring strategy.   Monitoring took place once a week by aircraft until it was determined to be out.

This fire was officially declared out on 10/15/15 at 0900 by Kings District FMO John Ziegler.

H552 reported two smokes in the Comb Creek area of Kings Canyon National Park, on 07/22/05. We had experienced a high volume of lightning strikes just prior to this. That coupled w/ the remote loctaion of the fire made this wilderness fire a good candidate for a wildland fire used for resource benefit. This fire, which was only 5 acres upon discovery, was adjacent to another smoke. The first smoke was called the Comb fire, the second, which was being reported on here was called Comb 2. Within one day the 2 fires had joined together and both were put under jurisdiction of several fire use teams, as the Comb Complex. The main part of the fire was actually Comb, not Comb 2. If you desire more information on the Complex, please refer to documentation for the Comb fire. Comb 2 was monitored by air until it became part of the Comb Complex, at which time numerous ground resources were assigned to monitor and herd the fire.

Lightning fire probably started during same storm that caused Boreal and Chagoopa fires. 1/2 mile north of Hockett fire.  Smoldering in heavy duff and litter with low spread potential.  Fire lined by crew members from Hockett fire. Fire was a full suppression strategy due to PL5.

Visitor came upon fire along Rae Lake loop trail and provided Garmin coordinates. Smoldering ground fire with a few bushes burning. 7000' elevation about 5 miles up Bubbs Creek trail. BC1 engaged H525 for recon who reported smoldering and some flame with low potential. Unable to locate point of origin. H525 was able to land aprx. 1 mile from fire. They saw smoldering fire, with some heavies burning, low spread potential. Fire must have been burning for a few days, found one campsite near fire that could have been point of origin, if fire continues to spread would move east and west along the river. No follow up after initial size-up with impending season ending weather event.

The Roads End fire was most likely intentionally started by visitors in a log  near the trail.  The first NPS employees on scene reported fire burning out    from several logs that were about 3 feet in diameter.  It was a windy day in   the canyon and the fire quickly spread through an open stand of young          ponderosa pine.  Two trails were closed temporarily,  one through the fire     area and one on the south side of the river due to the main fire spotting      across the river.  The park archaeologist surveyed the fire for possible       damage to known cultural sites from the fire or suppression actions and        reported that there was no damage.

The fire was then put into patrol status and was monitored by air until it was declared out on August 4th by Dave Allen.

THE CASTLE ROCK WILDFIRE BEGAN AS A RESULT OF ACCIDENTAL AERIAL IGNITION OUT-  SIDE THE CASTLE PRESCRIBED BURN UNIT.  CURRENT AND 24 HOUR FORECASTS INDICATED RISING HUMIDITIES SUFFICIENT TO PREVENT FIRE SPREAD IN GRASS, SO THE FIRE WAS  ALLOWED TO BURN.  AS EXPECTED, THE LOWER ELEVATIONS OF GRASS WERE EXTINGUISHED WITH THE NIGHT TIME RISE IN HUMIDITIES.  ONCE THE FIRE REACHED THE BLACK OAK   ZONE, NIGHT TIME HUMIDITIES WERE LOWER AND FAILED TO EXTINGUISH THE FIRE.      WITHIN 4 DAYS, THE MAJORITY OF THE WEST FACING SLOPES EAST OF DOME CREEK HAD   BURNED OUT.  IT BECAME EVIDENT THAT UNDER THE EXISTING AND FORECASTED WEATHER  PATTERN, THE FIRE WOULD CONTINUE TO FLANK TO THE EAST AND MAKE OCCASIONAL RUNS UPSLOPE TO CASTLE ROCKS.  CASTLE CREEK BECAME THE FIRST LOGICAL NATURAL        FEATURE TO CONTAIN THE EASTERN ADVANCE.  THE CULTURAL RESOURCE OFFICE          INDICATED THERE WERE NO KNOWN CULTURAL RESOURCES BETWEEN DOME CREEK AND CASTLE CREEK FROM THE MIDDLE FORK KAWEAH RIVER TO CASTLE ROCKS.  NIGHTTIME SMOKE      COMING DOWN CANYON TO ASH MTN AND THREE RIVERS BECAME A SERIOUS PROBLEM.  OVER THE COURSE OF SEVERAL WEEKS THE STATE POLLUTION STANDARD INDEX WAS EXCEEDED    SEVERAL TIMES INDICATING MODERATE TO UNHEALTHFUL AIR.  BETWEEN NOV. 16 & 28,   THE PARK RECEIVED NUMEROUS CALLS FROM THREE RIVERS RESIDENTS CONCERNING SMOKE  ISSUES.  OFFICIALS FROM THE SJVAPCB MET WITH FMO STAFF TO DISCUSS THE SMOKE    IMPACT ON THE THREE RIVERS.  ON NOV. 19, ONE TYPE II AND ONE TYPE III HELI-    COPTER WAS HIRED TO DROP WATER ON THE EASTERN FLANK, IN AN ATTEMPT TO RETARD   THE ADVANCE OF THE FIRE, AND TO REDUCE SMOKE PRODUCTION.  LATE OCT., NOV., AND EARLY DEC. PROVED TO BE THE DRIEST FALL IN OVER 50 YEARS.  BY DEC. 1ST, THE    EASTERN FLANK OF THE FIRE HAD REACHED THE LOWER ELEVATIONS OF CASTLE CREEK,    AND HAD BURNED THROUGH THE WESTERN 20% OF THE CASTLE SEQUOIA GROVE BELOW       CASTLE ROCK.  BOTH HELICOPTERS DROPPED WATER ALONG THE ENTIRE LENGTH OF THIS   EASTERN FLANK AND WERE SUCESSFUL IN CONTAINING THE FIRE WEST OF CASTLE CREEK.  THE SEASON'S FIRST MAJOR STORM BEGAN ON DEC. 11, 1995, WITH ASH MTN RECEIVING  .78" AND LODGEPOLE 2.20" OF PRECIPITATION.  ADDITIONAL RAIN AND SNOW OCCURED   ON DEC. 21 & 22, THUS, ENDING THE MONTH LONG SAGA OF  THE CASTLE ROCK WILDFIRE

This human caused fire was called in by Lodgepole housing resident 9215 as a fire in Lodgepole housing with a lot of smoke. Park Dispatch called out the structure brigade thinking it was a structure fire. They sent Lodgepole structure Engine 61, which is a Type 1 engine along with park rangers 9211, 613, 614, 617 and 513. Minutes later 9211 called back to park dispatch and stated that it was actually a grass fire prompting Ash Mountain Fire dispatch to send the Engine 51 module from Grant Grove. At 1703 the rangers stated over the radio that the fire was currrently out with no smoke showing but there was one smoldering log about 9 to 10 feet in length. The rangers also stated that a fire crew would be needed to continue to mop up. The Engine 51 module was en route from Grant Grove with their patrol vehicle with AFEO51 in charge. Rangers said they would assist the fire crew by letting them use water from structure Engine 61. At 1749 AFEO51 and the module arrived on scene and AFEO51 assumed IC for the incident. He described the fire as a 10 x 10 burning in one heavy timber, smoldering with containment lines and minimal spread potential. He also stated no additional resources were needed. After putting 500 gallons of water on the fire, the IC declared the fire contained with no smokes showing and stated it would be in patrol status. After a few days in patrol status by the Engine 51 module, the fire was officially declared out by Sequoia District FMO Robert Sanders on 7/28/16 at 1600. There was no official investigation on this fire but AFEO51 said that the fire was located behind quarters 81 in Lodgepole housing. He said it was a campfire that had escaped the ring.

Spread potential high, driving upslope into rocks with spread east and west. Concern is brushy fuels and Middle Fork trail with heavy smoke in canyon. Fire could push west down canyon and then run up Goddard Creek.  Hotshots committed for seven days with the objective of keeping the fire north of the Middle Fork of the Kings River.

On 7/23/06, following a period of severe fire weather and high lightning activity, backcountry ranger 115 found the Rae fire while out patrolling.  Upon discovery, the fire was .1 acres and was approximately 1/2 mile NE of the Rae lakes ranger station, burning in sparse whitebark pine fuels.  Ranger 115 estimated that the fire would burn itself out in a few days and was no threat to the cabin or nearby trail systems.  Based upon that, and the otherwise very remote location, District FMO Allen decided to use the fire for resource benefit.  We relied on Ranger 115 to update us and while we were out doing other recon work, we periodically flew by to monitor this small wilderness fire.  It was declared out on 8/15/06 after showing no growth or activity for quite some time.  Additional information can be obtained from folder CA-KNP-0035.

This lightning caused fire was originally reported by the park's 24 hour dispatch who received the message from the Porterville command center. The fire was described as being near Buck Rock Lookout in the Mitchell Peak area of Kings Canyon National Park. The park's H-552 was asked to do a recon of this fire once they arrived on duty. The fire was not suppressed but was monitored by air and mapped by ground personnel. This fire was officially declared out on 11/15/16 at 1200 by Kings Canyon District FMO John Ziegler.

On June 29, 04 Helicopter 552 was sent on a smoke recon of the Redwood Meadow  and Granite Creek area, we discovered a fire in the Granite Creek area.  Once  we returned to the base 552 crew was dispatched to the fire.  Rusty Coonfield, Larissa Swepston, Rya Krueger, and Fred Mason went to the fire.  Once we       landed we hiked over to the fire which was about 1/8 mile away from the LZ.    The fire was about 200 feet by 200 feet.  There was some active fire in the    heavy timber and smoldering duff.  The crew obtained the fire within a couple  hours of work.  Once the fire was contained we bone piled the remaining heavys to burn down.  On June 30, 04 we began to mop up the fire using dirt and H2O.  By the end of the day the fire was showing no smoke and was cold.  We hiked    back to the LZ and was picked up by the helicopter at 1730.

On 7/22/06, H552 discovered the Eagle fire (CA-KNP-0031) on a standard park recon following severe fire weather and lightning activity.  The fire, which was burning on the north side of Eagle Scout Creek was .25 acres upon discovery.  The lightning struck tree was on the ground and the resultant ground fire was moving to the south, primarily backing downhill.  Due to its location, management decided to suppress the Eagle fire, to avoid smoke drifting down valley into the neighboring town of Three Rivers, CA.  Eight members of C91, supported by various overhead such as helitack and fire dispatch, were flown out to the fire on 7/22/06 in the afternoon.  It had grown to .5 acres.  They lined the fire and initial attacked several spot fires that resulted from windy conditions.  On 7/24, they called the fire contained at 1.8 acres.  We continued to monitor the fire by air to ensure that it was out.  On 8/15, district FMO Dave Allen declared the fire out after a lengthy period of inactivity.

The fire was further described as being midway between Wormwood housing and Million Gallon Tank about 40 feet north of Manzanita trail. It was described as human caused and not near a powerline. Later, ranger 514 stated that from what he gathered by looking at the scene, it seemed that someone set up camp for the night there and had a little campfire. The individual(s) spread out sticks from the fire fire to create three little fires. He said the main fire was a 6x6 and the second one was 4x4. There was no description of the third fire. He asked Ash Mountain Fire Dispatch to order a Fire Investigator in ROSS but later that day cancelled the request. No further information has been given regarding the investigation performed. This fire was officially declared out by the Kings District FMO John Ziegler at 1700 on 8/29/16.

NP18146380 General Report Wildland Fire Three Graces, Sequoia National Park 8/26/2018 Officer Forrest Young, 679  At 1000 on 8/26/2018, Sequoia Dispatch advised me of smoke coming from the base of a Sequoia Tree near Upper Kaweah. I responded to the scene located at a group of trees known as The Three Graces and located Catherine Blanch, a shuttle ranger who took the initial report from visitors of smoldering duff. She stated She had already dumped a few buckets of water on the the patch of smoldering duff. The burned area was approximatly 2 square feet up to 6 inches deep at the base of the tree, the tree appeared unaffected. Blanch and I continued dumping water on it until it was cool to the touch and advised Sequoia and Ash Mountain Fire Dispatch the fire was out.

She asked if she needed to evacuate the visitors at Crystal Cave and shut down the tours. The Kings Canyon duty officer said an evacuation of Crystal Cave would not be necessary. Upon a recon of the area, the fire was said to be burning in timber, still one acre in size and smoldering. The fire was in the old Hidden fire scar to the east and up slope. This fire was officially declared out on 11/15/16 at 1200 by the Sequoia District FMO, Robert Sanders.

This lightning caused fire was discovered by visiting duty officer Chris Orr during a recon flight. At the time the fire was discovered it was contained in natural barriers and had a low growth potential. The plan was to monitor the fire by air. Over time, smoke sightings and reports came in from the area by rangers, hikers and other employees stating that smoke could still be seen from Road's End in Cedar Grove. The fire continued to smolder and experienced occasional roll-out on the slopes and began to grow in size. By 8/11 it was more than 30 acres in size. The fire eventually began to make it's way to the valley bottom and there was growing concern for the area hiking trails. Later in the month, fire activity began to increase and spread and ground crews were placed in the area for containment purposes. Of the ground crews were the Palomar Interagency Hotshots, E-43 from Yosemite National Park and a Wildland Fire Module from the Sequoia National Forest. H-552 provided 14 bucket drops on the fire. The trails were closed to through traffic and hikers were escorted through by firefighters. Containment and mop-up were continued through the month and by 8/31 all containment lines were holding and the trails had been re-opened for regular visitor use. The final acreage was 173. On 9/29 the Kaweah WFM hiked into the fire area to do a patrol after the Cedar Grove visitor center had a report of smoke and single log still burning. They found no visible heat or smoke and no threats to the line. The fire was officially declared out by DFMO Kelly Singer on 10/7/18 at 1300.

This was a vehicle fire reported by park employee 9A3 that began on the Generals Hwy just 1/4 mile below Amphitheatre point in the northbound lane. The entire perimeter of the car became engulfed and the fire began to move up the hillside and into the vegetation at a moderate rate of spread. An active column was visible with moderate smoke production from both fuel sources. Six Park Rangers responded along with Assistant FMO John Ziegler. Of those rangers that responded were 710, 713, 714, 513, 904, 717 and 711. A type 1 structure engine was dispatched to the scene along with type 3 E-72, C-91 and H-552. A type 3 engine from Calfire and a county water tender were also dispatched to the scene. H-552 performed a series of bucket drops that were very effective at initial attack. Once the vehicle was removed from the scene, wildland personnel continued to mop up and monitor the vegetation fire. The next day, E-72 patrolled the scene and the fire was declared controlled at 0939. The fire was officially declared out by AFMO John Ziegler on 6/23/17 at 1615.

This incident was a full suppression fire.  Initial report, by SQF Superintendant 3, reported it was on USFS land as a CA-SQF fire; however, the reported Lat/Long positioned it to be within the Park boundaries.  In addition to the above referenced resources, one Air Attack responded, AA15, rotating with AA410 and Lead Plane B58.  Referencing the above resource list, four helicopters, 2 type one (1) HT732 & HT795 and 2 type two (2) H520 & H522 responded initiating bucket drops.  Also, 2 Air Tankers, Tanker 22 & 55, responded initiating retardant drops.    Ground resources included KNP C-6, one module from SQF C-3, H522 Module, SQF E-31 and E-33 and SNF WT-2.  Additionally, a fire investigator was assigned; cause and blame is yet to be determined.    The fire was actively burning at a moderate rate of spread; however, suppression tactics held the fire at the top of the ridge burning approximately 6 acres.    NPS Sequoia-Kings National Parks took full accountability for this fire.  Further suppression details are on file in hand-written version submitted by data provider.

Fire reported to SEKI dispatch approximately 1100 hrs by H552.  SEKI severity  crew, Greg Funderburk (ICT-HAVO) +3, inserted to ridge above fire at 1500hrs.  Insertion of crew, and equipment required two helicopter trips.  Aeriel        reconnaissance conducted by IC en-route to fire.  Crew walked down south       facing ridge to fire at 9900 feet, S aspect, 40% slope.  Fire was burning in   light fuels with minimal potential for rapid spread.  Fire was 1/10 of an acre consuming 1000 hr fuels and creeping slowly through light fuels in Fuel Model  8.  Fire cause was a lightning strike to a Red Fir of 50-70 inch dbh.  Crew    began immediate scratch line around fuel while piling burning materials for    rapid consumption.  The top of the strike tree which was on the ground was     bucked and mopped up.  Weather upon crew arrival on scene at 1545 was light    rain and hail accompanied by intense lightning, OAT=45,RH=94.  Scratch line    completed around fire by 1700 OAT=43,RH=80.  Crew continued mopping up fire    until 2130, OAT=43, RH=68.  Winds were calm throughout operational period.     Crew hiked to helispot to camp and shift was completed at 2400.  Crew began    shift on 7/2/04 at 0600.  Evening mop up of ground fire was successful and     minimal additional mop up was needed.  Weather at 0700; OAT=44, RH=46, winds   calm, 0 CC.  Strike tree was burning at top and required falling for           extinguishment.  C-Faller requested at 0730 due to large size of tree.  C Fal- ler, (Jeff Pirog-YOSE), member of SEKI severity crew arrived on scene at 1030  via Helicopter 8CH.  Two members of IA module plus gear returned to Ash Mtn.   On the return flight.  Tree successfully felled at 1200.  Remaining members of IA crew mop up strike tree and extinguish fire by 1300.  Weather begins to     worsen by 1300 with threatening cumulus clouds.  Crew request extraction by    Helicopter 8CH at 1400.  Extraction requires two helicopter trips and 1 hour   for completion.  All crew members and equipment returned to Ash Mtn. by 1500.  Light rain falls on fire.  Crew makes initial report of Panther Fire on return trip to Ash Mtn Helibase.

This fire was reported by SEKI Park Ranger 710 and was called into the park's main 24 hour dispatch. At about 1335 on 4/4/15, the fire started close to the road above the Potwisha Grade on the Generals Hwy 198. The fire was creeping to the north between Potwisha Overflow parking and Hospital Rock in brush and oak. Traffic had to be closed both ways on the Generals Hwy due to the close proximity of the fire to the road and the fire activity. 710 initially responded with SEKI structure E-71 until CalFire resources could get on scene. The fire was directly suppressed using 2 Type 3 Calfire Tulare County Engines, 2 Helitankers dropping 6 cycles of water, and 2 Type 1 Crews from the surrounding area. Per the Sequoia District Ranger, Dave Fox, this fire is still currently under investigation by National Park Service Law Enforcement personnell and the case is still open. No official suspect findings have been made and are still unknown at this time. This fire was officially declared out by SEKI FMO Dave Allen on 6/7/15 at 1618.

On aug. 1,2001. @ approx. 1810 hrs. Ash Mtn. Fire Dispatch notified Dave       Bartlett, AFMO, that they had recieved a report of a wildland fire burning     near the Pan Point rd. Bill Wymore, Concession Manager @ Grant Grove reported  the fire.                                                                      Bartlett along w/ E-51 and P-51 responded. arriving @ scene @ appro. 1820      The fire was burning in thick duff(NFFL fuell model 8) @ approx. 400' west     of Pan Point rd. near the outer loop of crystal spring campground. Fire behav- ior was smoldering to creeping with flame lengths less then 1'. ROS's were les than 1ch/hr and initial sizewas less then 0.1 ac.                              E-51 quickly const. a wet line around the fire and reinforced the wetline      with a handline. Duff in the area was up to 1' thick and the fire required     extensive mop-up. The fire was contained @ 1835 hrs. and controlled @ 1845 hrs Units available @ 1930 hrs. The fire was rechecked for heat on aug. 2 and none was found. Fire was declared out on 8/3/2001.                                   INVESTIGATION: The fire appears to be human caused. The fire was @ the end of  social trail leading from the concesion dorm area. No evidence of any incend-  iary materail was found on the scene. Since the fire had burned deeply into th duff, it is likely that the fire had burned for some time, probably ignited    the previous evening or night and had smoldered for much of the day. The       proximity of the fire to the trail leads to the conclusion that the fire was   associated private social activities emanating from the concession dorm area.   No suspects or witness have been identified.

The Willow fire started shortly after we had experienced a period of moderate lightning activity.  It was discovered by H552 on 7/19 in the Willow Creek drainage area of Sequoia Ntl. Park.  The fire was .25 acres upon discovery and was burning on a SE aspect at 10,120 ft. in sparse fuels.  Because of its remote location, it was decided to use the fire for resource benefit.  Initially, we monitored the fire with aircraft but then eventually inserted fire monitors to map the fire and assess the fuels.  Though fuels were sparse and widely separated by rock outcroppings, single digit RH's allowed the fire to grow predominantly by spotting from one tree island to another.  In a short period of time the fire grew to 110 acres.  Soon therafter, we received a wetting rain which slowed the fire down.  By the time it was declared out on 11/06 the fire had grown to 185.0 acres.

On 8/3/05 at 1757 hours, a visitor reported a vehicle fire near the Hospital Rock area of Sequoia Ntl. Park. The threat of a wildfire was conveyed to the duty officer immediately and by 18:09 hours, overhead was on scene. The following resouces were then ordered: E72, C91, H552, E4162, and Patrol 32. All units, except E4162 which was cancelled before it got to the scene, began initial attack measures at the times stated above. By 18:19 the fire was contained. The vehicle fire had spread to nearby vegetation and had enormous potential for growth. Fortunately, the location that the flaming car came to rest was fortuitous. This combined w/ a rapid response from all units ensured that the fire was rapidly extinguished.

On 4/13/06 at 09:48 hrs., Ranger 8111 called in several smoke reports that she saw initiate from lightning strikes in the Eden Creek area of Mineral King. Greg Ver plus 2 firefighters were dispatched to confirm the report and indeed, 4 fires had started within close proximity to each other. This report details the events for CA-KNP-0001, the Eden #1 fire which was selected as an excellent candidate for a wildland fire used for resource benefit due to the remote location of the fire and very wet spring conditions.   The fire was monitored by ground crews until it was called out on 5/31/06 at 1500 hrs. by David Allen, fire use manager.

This human caused fire was reported by Ranger Alpha 62 as an abandoned campfire not within any kind of containment ring. The location was one of the  overflow pullouts at Wuksachi Village. Rangers 613 and 612 responded with a Type 1 structure engine and two non-firefighting personnel. Ranger 613 used a shovel to scratch line on sensitive boundaries. Ranger 613 said the fire appeared to be part of a large bonfire from the previous night, that had been dismantled. She said the logs were 2 to 3 feet in length and were actively burning with 6 inch flames. She said the spread potential was low and could be extinguished with 20-30 gallons of water. Captain 91 and 91 Zavala arrived on scene to continue with suppression and perform mop up. Captain 91 and 91 Zavala performed a cold trail of the area and declared the fire contained and controlled at 13:10. No known subjects were found and no known investigation was performed.

This lightning fire was reported by SEKI resource management employee Linda Mutch as a single foxtail pine near Mineral Peak on 7/27/15. The tree was smoking near the top with light smoke production at the base. SEKI Helicopter 552 performed a reconnaissance flight and found no smoke visible in the area. The following day, SEKI Backcountry Ranger 9-2-69 reported a smoldering log on the ground 3/4 of a mile west of Monarch Lakes approximately 50 feet below the trail. The ranger stated it was possibly the same smoke. Subsequent recon flights of the area revealed no smoke showing. A final recon flight on 8/10/15 showed no smoke visible with no growth. This fire was officially declared out by SEKI FMO Dave Allen on 8/10/15.

This human-caused fire was reported by Cedar Grove campground host 4120 as a 15 foot downed log with fire burning inside and underneath it. The fire was located in Sentinel Campground site 78. There were no fire resources located in Cedar Grove at the time due to the Sherman RX. Cedar Grove Maintenance worker 463 overheard the traffic on Command and notified Ash Mountain Fire Dispatch that he had a trailer with several gallons of water and would extinguish the fire for us. 4120 remained on scene to help as well. At 1448, 463 reported the log fire was extinguished and out. A few days later, FMO John Ziegler went to patrol the fire and saw no concerns. This fire was officially declared out by DFMO Kelly Singer on 10/17/19 at 1000. As far as we know, there was no official investigation by Law Enforcement on this fire and no known suspects have been identified.

H-552 discovered the Alta South fire while doing a trail crew resupply.  The fire was discovered on 7/11/08, after a lightning storm.  The struck tree had spread fire to the ground to a 20 bt. by 20 ft. area, which was smoldering.  Due to the sparseness of fuels, it was given a low potential for growth on the upslope side of the fire, however, it did have moderate potential downslope where fuels were more continuous.  Due to its remote location, it was decided to monitor this fire.  It was monitored by air until it was declared out on 10/05/08 by DFMO Allen.

This fire was reported by Lodgepole ranger 612 to Park Dispatch around 1:37 AM. Park dispatch called the on-call fire dispatcher(ECC1) at home to let her know.  ECC1 then called the duty officer who asked her to have SEKI Engine 51 respond to the scene. Lodgepole rangers 612, 6-310, and 617 began and completed line construction around the fire and told Park Dispatch that Engine 51 could stand down. On Aug. 2nd, 2003, a small column of smoke was reported by NPS visitors on the NW aspect of Redwood Mountain.  The decision was made tosuppress the fire due  to high fire danger and the fire being in close proximity to the Park Service  boundary.  The Kings Canyon engine 51 crew was sent to suppress the fire.  The fire was a single snag burning in the top and spreading very slowly through    the ground fuels around it.  Helicopter 214HM was utilized to put buckets on   the tree to cool it down.  The helicopter was released after 8 drops.  A class C faller was called in the next day to drop the tree from Arrowhead Hotshots.  Helicopter 552 shuttled the faller plus two in the next day.  The tree was     successfully dropped and handline was constructed to contain the fire.  This   fire was declared out on Sept. 15, 2003.

On Sept. 26, 2005, a smoke report was called into Ash Mt. fire dispatch by the Buck Rock lookout.  Approximately one hour later, helicopter 552 did a recon of the area to confirm the smoke report, which placed the fire near Tar Gap, in the Mineral King drainage of Sequoia Ntl. Park.  The fire was located on the NW facing slope just below Tar Gap.  It was approximately 1000 ft. above the Tar Gap trail at an elevation of @8800 ft.  The fire was confirmed to be located within the Tar Gap prescribed burn that was conducted in 2002.  Fire activity remained minimal until the fire was declare out on Oct. 27, 2005 at a total of 1.0 acre.

This wildland fire originated as part of a structure fire that was given the incident name of Wilsonia. Park Dispatch called the fire dispatch emergency line at 1930 on 8/19/16 and left a message that said there was a wildland fire in Wilsonia, near Grant Grove Village. They stated the fire was in the grass and spreading quickly and that wildland fire resources needed to get en route. However, they never mentioned the incident was initially a structure fire. Ash Mountain Fire Dispatch went into service and overheard via the radio that the structure on fire was an old privately owned cabin that was fully engulfed. It was also stated that nearby grass and trees had become enflamed. Park Dispatch had dispatched the Grant Grove structure Engine 52 and had called neighboring county and Forest Service units. Wildland resources from the park had been stood down per the FMO due to it primarily being a structural fire. The next day SEKI Engine 41 responded to patrol the area and check for any continued burning or smoldering vegetation. They were asked not to interfere with the burned area as it would be treated as a crime scene and formally investigated. The structure was later identified as the old Johnson Cabin and the Society of Historic Preservation Office had been notified. This fire is still currently under investigation and no further details have been provided. In order to prevent confusion, the wildland portion of this incident was named Johnson. This fire was officially declared out by Kings District FMO John Zielger on 8/29/16 at 1700.

The Fire was first sighted on 10/2/2002 but was not suppressed right away.     This was due to it being late in the day and the weather compromising FF       safety.  Crew 91 with 8 people initial attacked the fire on 10/03/2002.  We    flew in at 0930 and arrived at the Fire at 1030.  The fire was burning in      heavy dead and down brush.  We briefed and started line construction at 1035.  We had the fire contained at 1420.  We then started to mop-up.  The fire was   burning slowly due to the wet fuels from the weather the night before.  I      (Will Basye) released D.Turley and B. Wilson at 1730 to return to Ash Mtn.     The rest of the crew of 6 continued to mop-up until 2200.  The next day we     started at 0600.  The fire was still actively burning in the large logs.  By   0800 we had fire under control.  We stayed on the fire through the day and     left at 1700.  We arrived at the vehicles at 1830 and left for Ash Mtn.         H552 also did troop shuttles and sling loads.

On 7/26/06 Dave McCandliss from Sierra Ntl. Forest contacted our fire dispatch office.  Evidently his air attack had spotted a fire that was close to the USFS/NPS boundary line near the Obelisk, in Kings Canyon Ntl. Park.  There was a good bit of confusion surrounding the ownership of the fire but it was finally decided that it belonged to the Ntl. Forest.  They thus opened a firecode for the incident and sent a crew of 4 helitack out to suppress the fire on 7/27/06.  The crew called in new coordinates and for the second time we plotted the fire in wildcad and told them that it appeared to be on NPS property.  One of the crew members felt certain that the GPS readout was wrong so they continued to suppress the fire as if it were on USFS land.  Later it was realized that the fire was actually a NPS fire.  The suppression effort was however complete and Sierra Ntl. Forest called the fire contained on 7/27.  The Sierra Ntl. Forest helitack crew demobed later that day.  Since it was our fire, we chose to write the 1202 to document the actions taken.  Though we charged nothing against it, we also opened our own firecode.  The fire was declared out on 7/30/06 by NPS FMO Dave Bartlett after consulting with the Ntl. Forest.

This fire was caused by a lightning strike on a dead tree at the Red Fir Maintenance Facility near Wuksachi in Sequoia National Park. This fire was burning at 0.1 acres in timber and had a low spread potential when discovered due to precipitation in the area. Direct suppression was utilized due to structures in the vicinity and the possiblity of higher spread potential as conditions dried out. SEKI resources responding to this incident were Type 3 Engine 51, 6 members of the Arrowhead IHC and 2 members of the helitack crew.

This lightning caused fire was reported by back country Ranger 745 as smoke across the canyon from the helispot on Mineral King Road. A park visitor had also reported to the Mineral King ranger station that smoke was visible on mile marker 14 on the right hand side of the road, light grey and flame was visible from turn. H-552 performed a recon of the area and reported the fire burning in timber and brush, including bear clover and manzanita at 2 acres. Due to high spread potential and the location of the fire, initial attack via air resources was quickly underway. Of the aerial resources ordered for the fire was H-552 and H-523 from Porterville. AA-410 and AA-15 as well as 7 Air Tankers from Porterville, also provided aerial suppression support. A type 2 IMT was called in to manage the fire and the Sequoia National Forest ECC in Porterville took over expanded dispatch operations while Ash Mountain Fire Dispatch remained the aircraft ordering and management point. Of the ground resources who provided suppression for this fire were: Folsom Lake IHC, Los Padres IHC, Little Tujunga IHC, Kings River IHC and Palomar IHC, Chico Crew Camps 26 and 28, E-42 and E-44 from SQF, E-73 from CA-ANF and E-43 from Yosemite National Park. This fire was officially declared out by DFMO Kelly Singer on 10/7/18 at 1300.

This wildfire was discovered by park Law Enforcement Rangers during a SAR on 4/13/17 at 2300 PM.  A missing hiker was in the area of Potwisha on a fishing trip when he became lost from his party. Once rangers reached him he had started a pretty large warming fire. Rangers 712 and 710 doused the fire with water but had no tools to scratch line. The next morning the captain of the fuels crew patrolled the area and found no smoke showing and the site was completely cold. The suspect is known but no known arrests have been made. This fire was officially declared out by Kings District FMO, John Ziegler on 4/17/17 at 1300.

On June 30, 2002, Park Ranger Justing Iverly received a visitor reprt of       smoke in the vicinity of the Motor Trail in Cedar Grove.  Iverly investigated  and at 1205 hours discovered a wildland fire burning betweeen the Motor Trail  and the River approximately 3/4 mile west of Cedar Graove Fire Station.  The   fire initially reported to be 1/4 to 1/2 acre, burning in NFFL Fuel Model 9    with a moderate rate of spread and occasional torching.                        Initial response was by NPS Engine 51, USFS e 31, USFS E32, USFS handcrew      Black Eagle 1, NPS H552, USFS Battalion Chief 31 and NPS DIV 2.  Initial       actions were to construct wetline around the fire, supported by H552.  USFS    E 31 and BC 31 were released at 1440 hours.  The wetline was subsequently      reinforced by handline and fire was contained at 1614 hours.  Patrol and       mop-up continued until dark and units all were released by 2030.                Mop-up and patrol continued on JULy 1 by NPS E 51 supported by Blackeagle hand crew.  Mop-up was complete by 1630.                                             Initial investigation into fire cause was inconclusive with no clear evidence  to identify the cause of the ignition.  The fire appears to be human caused    however, no clear ignition device or method could be identified. Investigation is continuing under the authority of Cedar Grove Sub-district Ranger.
THIS PNF WAS IN A AREA THAT HAD PREVIOUSLY BURNED IN THE 1970'S AND 80'S.      NO PROBLEMS OR SMOKE COMPLAINTS LOGGED OR RECORDED.

This lightning fire was discovered by SEKI Helicopter 552 during a reconnaisance flight over the Sequoia District. The fire was burning in timber at 0.1 acres with low spread potential. The fire was monitored by air due to low spread potential and no threat to resources and was declared contained, controlled and out by SEKI FMO on 8/11/15 at 14:00.

Ranger 612 reported a fire in the Upper Sherman Tree parking area on 8/18.  Maintenance personnel and a local ranger witnessed a youg boy running away from a rock slab where he had started a 10 ft. by 10 ft. blaze.  Though a callout was made to both engines 51 and 72, local park personnel had extinguished the blaze by the time the engines had arrived.  Fire personnel remained on scene to mop up the incident, with Ramirez as IC.  Attempts were made to figure out who the boy was, but due to the high volume of visitor traffic in this area as well as his speedy retreat, the fire starter's identity remains unknown.  The arson fire was declared out by duty officer John Ziegler, on 8/19, at a total of .1 acres.
Lightning fire with low spread potential and low fire behavior. Fire was monitored by air due to low values at risk and remoteness.

Helitack crew 552 was dispatched on the Gnat fire on 9/21/05.  The crew hiked to the fire and worked it from 1900-2100, on the 21st of September.  The fire was a lightning caused fire that had been burning for a while.  There was a single standing snag and .25 acres of ground fire.  Most of the fire was cold.  The following day the crew worked the fire again putting out all smokes and cooling all hot spots.   The crew watched the fire through the burn period and then placed it in patrol status.  Helitack crew was picked up at 1600 hours on 9/22/05.

This fire was ignited by an abandoned vehicle which caught on fire just below the Eleven Range location on Generals Highway.  A fire brigade for the vehicle was dispatched in addition to wildland fire resources.  Helicopter 552 contributed a brief recon while in route to base for bucket retreival.  Upon arrival of the engine and crew, it was determined that this fire lacked the need for a bucket drop due to the fact that it was surrounded by rock and was suppressed easily.

On 7-12-01 at 0830 a family reported that they watched a fire for three days   before they could report it.  The fire was east of Helon Lake.  Helicopter     552 reconned the fire.  The fire was less than .1 acres.  The fire was         burning in steep Rocky terrain with low potential of growth.

On July 2, H-8CH discovered a new smoke at Panther Gap area.  Due to the       weather we decided not to attack the fire till July 3.  On the morning of July 3 three firefighters were dropped off at Panther Meadow by H552.  We hiked     into the fire and arrived at about 1030 am.  The three of us dug line around   the fire and contained it within a few minutes.  There was on C class tree to  be dropped, and a request for a C faller was made.  At around 1310 Ben Jacobs  and Clint Coonfield arrived at the LZ.  About a hour later they arrived at the fire.  They sized up the tree and dropped it.                                   Hike out of the fire to the wolverton trail head.  Where we were picked up and drove back to Ash Mountain and Grant Grove.                                     The fire was 20x20 feet in size.  There was smoldering fire behavior in the    duff.  With light wind, temps were 58 and RH of 70.

This fire was reported by a Three Rivers resident as smoke near Alta Peak. H-552 performed a recon of the area and determined the fire to be lightning-caused on the ridge west of Alta Peak. The fire was burning in heavies and in scattered trees burning low to ground cover. There was low to moderate fire behavior with rollout off the ridge to timber below. There was no direct suppression of this fire and it was monitored by air.
Dead and down log intentially set on fire.  When discovered, log was smoldering.  Suppression tactics were taken.

On 7/21/2006, H552 discovered a lightning caused fire SW of Burnt Mountain.  Upon discovery, the fire was .1 acres.  Due to its remote location duty officer Dave Bartlett decided to use the fire for resource benefit.  As a result, we staffed the fire with fire monitors and members of the fire effects crew.  It was also supported by helitack and Helicopter 552, various overhead performing administrative duties, and E-51.  The fire grew slowly, with backing and flanking being the predominant fire behavior.  At its conclusion it had reached 627.0 acres.  Dave Bartlett, our FMO, declared the fire out on 12/29/06.  Additional information can be obtained under fire folder CA-KNP-0026.

The Slide fire was started by lightning probably around August 1, 2002.  The   fire was discovered by park helicopter 552 on 8-6-2002 at 1252 hrs.  The fire  was burning in above the main portion of Slide Bluffs on the Middle Fork of    the Kings River.  Fuels were sparse, generally described as SEKI custom NFFL   Fuel model 18 hgih altitude short needle conifer.  Initial fire spread was     slow due to the sparse fuels and fire behavior could best be described as      creeping or smoldering.                                                         Although the fire had the potential for significant uphill growth, past fire   history in the area made it unlikely that the fire would reach a large size.   There were no values at risk.  The decision was made to manage the fire.        The fire continued to burn throughour the summer.  Fire behavior remained      smoldering to creeping , although occassion torching was observed in heavier   pockets of standing timber.  Most fire spread was log-to-log along the heavier down fuels.                                                                     The fire continued to burn until the season ending event on November 10, 2002.

On 8/22/05, crew memebers from E-51 discovered a smoke in the maintenance dump site, in the Grant Grove area of Kings Canyon Ntl. Park. The fire, which was less than .1 acres upon discovery resulted from sawdust that had ignited while maintenance crews were using chainsaws in the vicinity. Due to the fact that the fire was very near both buildings and hazardous materials,  it was promptly put out on the spot. It did not grow past the .1 acre size and was declared out on 9/16/05. No injuries were incurred during its suppression. Internal tracking number was 58, CA-KNP number was 0031.

This was a lightning caused fire located Southwest of Little Baldy in Grant Grove. The fire was 3/4 of a mile from the General's Highway 198, smoldering and burning at the base of a snag and had a moderate to high spread potential. Though no structures were immediately threatened, the fire did pose a threat to road traffic and people. Sequoia National Forest Engine 47 was covering that day and responded to provide full suppression due to the location and spread potential. This fire was officially declared out by Sequoid District FMO Robert Sanders on 10/14/15 at 1200.

This fire was reported by Park Ridge Lookout as a downstrike in the Yucca Mountain area that produced smoke. H-552 performed a recon of the area and described the fire as burning in mixed conifer, clover and manzanita with trees candling. Spread potential was low to moderate. A cycle of bucket drops were provided and firefighters were inserted for initial attack. H-552 did 7 water drops for a total of 1008 gallons using a 144 gallon bucket.

On 7/23/06, following a period of severe fire weather and high lightning activity, backcountry ranger 116 found the Roaring fire not far from the Roaring River Ranger Station.  Upon discovery, the fire was .1 acres burning on a steeply sloped, north aspect at 7770 ft.  Due to the remoteness of the location, District FMO Allen decided to use the fire for resource benefit.  It continued to back and flank growing quite slowly over the next several months.  On 8/7/06, another lightning strike to the SW of the Roaring fire was discovered.  The Ridge fire also became a fire use fire.  It was evident that in time both fires would grow together.  On 9/25 they did just that.  At the time they joined, Ridge was 139.0 acres.  Roaring finally attained an acreage of 1643.0, making the Roaring-Ridge "complex" a total of 1782.0 acres.  Since we have reported on the Ridge fire elsewhere, we are simply reporting the Roaring acres here.  As Roaring grew, some pre-emptive actions were taken by the Arrowhead Hotshots to help protect a water system that supplies Cedar Grove.  Numerous other resources, as listed above, also helped to monitor the fire and supply it, as well as to accomplish the necessary administrative functions associated with a large fire use event. On October 9th, 2002, NPS Helicopter 552 observed smoke coming fro the Redwood Mountain area of King Canyon National Park. Upon investigation, they           discovered that a single monarch Giant Sequoia tree had been struck by         lightning and was burning in both the crown and adjacent ground fuels.         Arrowhead IHC and NPS E-51 were dispatched, arriving on-scene at approxiamtely 1200 hrs.  Fire behavior was mostly smoldering to creeping with slow rates of  spread.  The fire in the Giant Sequoia was inaccessible and the tree was       likely to continue to spread buring material throughout the area.  Adjacent    fuels consisted of heavy down timber best described as NFFl Fuel Model 10.     (FRID class Extreme)                                                           Because of air quality and other concerns, initial actions were taken to       prevent the rapid spread of the fire until an analysis of the situation could  be completed.  Small check lines were completed to slow spread of the fire     into areas of heavier fuels.  Stage 1 of the wildland fire implementation plan was developed and completed by 1400 hrs. on October 9, 2002 and work was       completed on Stage 2 of the Wildland Fire Implementation Plan by 1800 hrs.      The Whiskeytown fire use Module was assigned to monitor the fire on October 10 2002.  Daily on-the-ground monitoring of the fire continued through October 14 and the fire was monitored primarily by air through the rest of the burning    season.  Fire spread remained limited-primarily halted by check lines. The     fire was declared out after a season ending event on November 11, 2002.

The timber fire was discovered on 11/17/05 by fire monitor Erdody.  On 11/27/05 the fire was flown and was estimated to be 3.0 acres in size, burning in red fir, fuel model 8.  Smoldering, creeping and backing were the primary types of fire behavior noticed.  Since we had recently done a prescribed burn not far from where the timber fire was found, we made the incorrect assumption that this was a spot fire off of Highbridge East.  Consequently, 3 firefighters from E72 were dispatched to put the fire out.  Inclement weather dampened any chances of it coming back to life and the fire was officially declared out on 12/02/05.

This fire was called in by the park 24 hour dispatch as an active fire at Wolverton turnoff near the Upper Sherman parking lot in the Giant Forest. Park dispatch called out their structural fire brigade to assist, which consisted of one Type 1 Engine and three park rangers. It was reported as approximately 30 x 30 in size with minor active flame at the base of a couple of trees. Rangers provided initial attack directly on the flames with handtools. Of the wildland resources that responded were E-72 and the Arrowhead hotshots. The fire was located directly between the Wolverton quarry and the Upper Sherman parking lot and was creeping in deep duff with low fire activity. Scanning of the area revealed a fire ring at the scene. Direct suppression techniques were performed due to the location of the fire and its close proximity to visitor populated areas. This fire was routinely investigated by the park law enforcement staff but no subject has been identified at this time. This fire was officially declared out by the Sequoia District FMO, Robert Sanders on 11/6/16 at 1332.

The Comb fire was discovered by H552 in the Comb Creek are of Kings Canyon National Park, on 7/22/05.  There were two original lightning strikes which burned together.  (The 2nd strike, known as Comb 2, has been reported on elsewhere.)  The remote location of the fire made it a good candidate for a wildland fire used for resource benefit.  The fire, which was .5 acres upon discovery, eventually grew to a total of 8675.0 acres on NPS land and 1079.0 acres on USFS land.  It was managed cooperatively between the two agencies as a fire use project.  Due to the size and complexity of the fire, it was managed by two fire use management teams.  Resources that helped to hold and monitor the fire included: 2 Fire Use Mngt. Teams Saguarro Fire Use Module CA-KNP Fire Monitors 2 Stanislaus (USFS) Fire Use Modules Bandelier Fire Use Module Arrowhead Hotshots Crew 91 Crew 7 (CA-YNP) 5 helicopters E-51 E-72 Miscellaneous overhead (helibase manager, base camp mangager, etc.)    Operations went well, though holding actions were necessary at several trigger points to avoid sensitive boundaries and the Cedar Grove area.  All objectives were met and no property losses or injuries occurred.  The fire accomplished many resource benefits such as stand density reduction, hazard fuel reduction and habitat improvement.  Voluminous records are available on operational details in the original fire folders should that information be needed.  Total cost ran approximately $1,350,000.00 which averages out to $138.40 per acre for both the USFS and NPS acres.  The fire was declared out on 12/01/05.

The following changes were made to this Fire Report as part of the conversion from SACS to WFMI on 05/14/2005. TYPE INIT ATTACK 4: S - Mapped to W End of SACS to WFMI conversion notes.  On october 4, 2002 while performing normal duties I(Larry Smith) received      from ash mtn fire dispatch a reprt of an actively burning wildfire.  The       initial report was received at 1000 hrs. from a visitor who reported the fire  as located one half mile south of the wye (intersection of CA 180 and Generals Hwy.) Initial response consisted of 562 in a type 6, engine and 512 in a Law   Enforcement patrol vehicle.  At 1030 hrs 562 and 512 arrived on scene, which   was actually 3.7 miles south of the wye.  562 assumed IC and began initial     size up while 512 provided traffic control.                                    Initial size up showed the fire to be one tenth of an acre creeping in 3ft     manzanita and needle cast with occasional active flames and isolated torching. Fire cause was from a lightning struck tree adjacent to the fire.  Fire was    deep seated and was assumed to have been ignited during a lightning storm that occurred on Oct 1.                                                             At 103.7 562 initiated attack utilizing a one and one half-inch hose at 120gpm at 1040 SQF Patrol 32 arrived with one on board and assited with the           suppression action.  At 1045 560 arrived and assumed IC.  After conferring     with 562, 560 requested a short squad of Arrowhead hotshots equipped with      chainsaws for assisting mop-up.  At 1100 six Arrowheads arrived led by Saw     Boss Gerwe.  Arrowhead cut an improved handline and assisted with mop-up       operations.  At 1315 hrs. 560 turned IC to 562 and returned to station.  At    1400 hrs.  562 released all resources and placed the fire in patrol status.    Fire was declared out on Nov. 9th by 560.

This backcountry fire was reported by backcountry Ranger 116 during her patrol. She claimed visitors had reported to her a small fire smoldering deep into the ground 200 yards east of Sand Flat Junction. She stated that visitors were trying to put it out and that it was on the south side of the trail. A visitor had marked the area with orange rope and the fire site was between Sand Flat Junction where Bullfrog Lake can be seen. Ranger 116 hiked over to the site to investigate the fire and stated that visitors had dug a deep trench with a mound in the middle that looked like a volcano but saw no signs of any visitors. H552 did a recon of the area and inserted two helitack crewmembers who performed mop-up at the site. This fire was officially declared out by Kings District FMO John Ziegler on 9/23/16 at 1507.

The Rock Creek wildfire was reported at 1152 hrs on 9-18.  The fire was        located on the east side of Sequoia national Park. The best estimate of the    casue of the fire was determined to be from an abondoned campfire.  Fules were primarily grass and the fire was inactive when four helitack personnel arrived on scence.  Due to the remoteness of the location, high elevation, time of     year, and the presence of natural barriers surrounding the fire it was decided to manage the fire under a confinement strategy.  The fire was checked again   on 9/21 and had experienced no growth. The fire was checked for the last time  on 9/28 with no smokes showing.

The following changes were made to this Fire Report as part of the conversion from SACS to WFMI on 05/14/2005. TYPE INIT ATTACK 1: R - Mapped to T End of SACS to WFMI conversion notes.  On August 21, 2003 the West Kern fire started on the west side of the Kern     River Canyon about 1.5 miles from the boundary with Sequoia National Forest.   No significant fire history from the last 100 years has been recorded in this  area.                                                                           No lare fire use projects had been authorized in the last 25 years, due to the fact the Sequoia National Forest lacked a fire use program.  2003 marked the   first year of a fire use program for the Sequoia National Forest.  Therefore,  in conjunction with the Sequoia National Forest (SQF) the W. Kern fire was     managed as a fire use project.  In addition, the fire was managed with the     Inyo National Forest.                                                           The West Kern fire burned for 120 days for a final acreage of 7968 acrews.     The fire was managed by fire use team Bonefeld and Cook.                        FUMA:  DAVE ALLEN (SEKI)                                                              BILL KAAGE (SEKI)                                                              PAUL GIBBS (SQF)                                                         Acreage:  Sequoia and Kings NP:  3510 acres                                              Sequoia National Forest:  2474 acres                                           Inyo National Forest:  1984 acres                                     Total Acreage:  7968 acres

Ranger 6112 discovered the remains of human caused fire in the redwood meadow backcountry camping area on the Middle Fork of the Kaweah by Cliff Creek.
