The lightning caused Hidden fire was discovered on Sept. 10th, late in the day, by a park fire monitor.  At the time of discovery, the fire was .25 acres burning in rugged country in fuel model 8.  Because of its location and the vast amount of fuel in the area, management decided to suppress the fire.  On Sept. 11th, an initial attack crew of 14 Arrowhead hotshots was dispatched to put out the fire along w/ P71.  The fire escaped initial attack so on the 12th, DFMO Allen took over as ICT3 at 11 acres.  Air support was requested and rendered but the fire was not caught.  By the 13th, the fire was 60 acres and at 50% containment.  Additional crews were ordered to support the effort.  Steve Barran took over as ICT3 on the 14th and a type 2 long team was ordered.  By the 16th, at 1800 hours, Joseph Carlton's team had assumed command of the fire which was by that point approximately 400 acres in size.  Because of the difficulty of the terrain, a decision was made to quit doing direct attack which was not working despite incredible efforts by a a plethora of aircraft and crews (which include 7 additional type 2 ships, 1 additional type 3 ship, numerous air tankers and lead planes above and beyond what is documented in the fire mangt. data section.)  Instead, the team, with the park's blessing, fell back to a more defensible position.  Line was cut around the fire in areas where it was likely to hold and crews then begain firing off of those lines, thus allowing the interior fuel to be consumed.  Nightly infrared flights watched for spots duirng the burnout operation and crews were swift to pick up errant spot fires.  This series of actions allowed for less resource damage via all of the retardant that was being dropped to no avail while removing fuel from a known, troublesome area, while providing for better firefighter safety.  When the type 2 team departed, Chuck (William) Heard assumed command as ICT3.  On Dec. 9th, DFMO Allen called the fire out at 3685 acres at a cost of approximately 9 million dollars.

Lightning ignited a small fire southeast of Kettle Dome and about 3/4 of a mile from the NPS/USFS boundary, on 8/31.  Fire monitors were flown into the fire to do an assessment of nearby fuels, trails and the likelihood of the fire reaching the USFS boundary prior to cool fall weather approaching.  Based upon the intelligence they brought back from the field, it was decided that this fire was a good candidate for a fire being used for resource benefit as it had a low potential for growth.  Occasional helicopter overflights were subsequently used to map and monitor the fire.  It was also checked again by a fire effects crew prior to it being called out on 9/23 by DFMO Rosso, after receiving precipitation.

This human caused fire was reported to the park's 24 hour dispatch by backcountry ranger 8-1-15 as an escaped campfire at Eagle Lake. She described the fire as 10 feet by 3 feet in size and burning at the base of a downed tree. The ranger was instructed via radio by the helibase captain to pour water at the base of the fire. Three members of the park helitack crew and four other firefighters were inserted by helicopter into the area to provide direct attack on the fire. Size up revealed the fire to be human caused burning in heavies and a lot of dead and downed timber and litter. The rough size of the fire was a quarter of an acre and was smoldering with a low spread potential. This fire was routinely investigated by the park's law enforcement personnel but no subjects are known at this time. This fire was officially declared out by Sequoia District FMO, Robert Sanders on 11/6/2016 at 1332.

The Hidden fire, which is located to the SE of Hidden Spring, in Sequoia Ntl. Park was caused by lightning on 10/31 and discovered on Nov. 1st.  At the time of discovery it was .1 acres in size in a transitional ecotone at 5650 ft.  The area is the blending zone for the upper reaches of foothills vegetation and the lower extent of the mixed conifer forest, thus vegetation is quite thick and continuous on several planes.  As a result of the difficult access to the fire, the lateness of the season, and several bouts of inclement weather, it was decided to manage the fire for resource benefit, thus reducing firefighter risk.  The fire was monitored by air until it was declared out by DFMO Allen at 3.0 acres.

On July 18, 2005 Ranger 9232 Karplus discovered a fire in the Volcanic Lakes area of Kings Canyon Ntl. Parks. She radioed in a report of a .1 acre fire burning at approximately 9700feet in lodgepole and whitebark pine duff. The fire was exhibiting open flames and smoldering activity. Upon investigation it appeared that the fire was started from an escaped campfire, thus was human caused. Duty officer Jacobs decided to supress the fire and on 8/19/05, 2 firefighters were dispatched to the scene. They wet-mopped the fire and by 8/20/05, the fire was called contained. Nicole Ver performed as ICT5 trainee on the incident. No injuries incurred.

A park visitor reported this fire to the 24 hour Park Dispatch of the Fallen Goliath tree burning in Redwood Canyon. Fire personnel hiked into the area for initial attack and determined the fire to be human caused. There was no evidence of a lightning strike and the fire was burning inside the fallen tree. The fire was also smoldering in the underbrush and the spread potential was moderate. Suppression crews worked to remove heavy fuels around the tree. Fire burning inside the tree made suppression difficult due to chimney effect. A mule pack train was used to haul water and supplies into the fire to aid in suppression tactics.

Packers working for Charlie Mills reported an escaped campfire to him on 7/10/06.  Charlie, in turn, called and gave us the report on this .1 acre fire that his employees found by Lost Lakes, in Kings Canyon Ntl. Park.  Evidently while coming down the hiking trail, they saw an escaped campfire that was roughly 5 ft. by 10 ft.  They decided to stop and line the fire before heading into the office.  When Charlie called us, duty officer Jacobs decided to dipatch 3 firefighters to the scene to ensure that the fire had been completely extinguished.  H552 flew McKibben, Ramirez and Elles out to search for the fire on the following day.  They were unsuccessful and ended up spending the night.  On the following day, they found the .1 acre burn and ensured that it was cold.  McKibben acted as ICT5 on the fire; Ramirez was her trainer.  Elles went as FFT2.  The fire was declared out on 7/13/2006 after showing no growth.

The Granite fire started by lightning on July 29. The fire was detected by the Bear Paw back country ranger. A recon of the fire was completed by H552 and Duty Officer David Allen. The fire was staffed via helicopter with  Crew 91 and two members of helicopter 552. The fire was lined the first night with three days of mop up.

H552 reported a smoke in the Roaring River area of Kings Canyon National Park, on 8/16/05. We had experienced a high volume of lightning strikes just prior to this. That Coupled w/ the remote location of the fire made this wilderness fire good candidate for a wildland fire used for resource benefit. Since we had a fire use team in the park at the time, they agreed to do the stage 1 report. The fire activity, which was very low in intensity, was monitored via aircraft. By 8/18/05 no smoke was showing in the lightning struck tree. No appreciable growth occured and the fire was declared out on 9/16/05 after a lengthy period of inactivity.

This fire was monitored by air due to low spread potential and no threat to resources. The Buck Rock fire lookout called in a lightning caused fire near Mitchell Peak on the 1st of Oct.  We sent two firefighters into the area to size up the fire.  It was .1 acres in size with a moderate potential for growth.  The fire was managed under modification to the implementation of the Ntl. Fire Policy.  Based upon the fire's location and the time of season, management decided to take no direct suppression actions.  The fire stayed at .1 acres and was declared out on Oct. 11th by DFMO Rosso.  The .1 acres that were burned will be used for resource benefit.

This was an abandoned campfire reported by Park Ranger 4-1-2. He received reports the fire was smoldering in the sand flats on the Bailey Bridge Trail about 1.5 miles from the Roads End Trailhead in Cedar Grove. H-552 did a reconnaissance flight over the area and flew in 4 helitack crewmembers for initial attack. Upon arrival, the IC reported the fire had already been lined and contained by hikers. From that point the helitack crew peformed a mop up of the fire. H-552 did 3 water drops totaling 540 gallons using a 180 gallon bucket.

On 8/27/06, while out doing an aerial recon, Dave Allen discovered that the USFS Tamarack fire had crossed over the NPS boundary in the vicinity of Coyote Pass and Tamarack Creek.  It was decided to use the encroaching fire for resource benefit.  At the time of discovery it was 1 acre in size.  We monitored the fire by air until it was declared out on 10/16.  At that time it had shown no appreciable growth since it was first discovered which was not surprising due to the sparseness of fuels in the area.

This fire was officially declared out by acting FMO, John Ziegler, on 10/17/17 at 1151.

Resource Type U: Helicopter 552 Resource Type Z: One Duty Officer via helicopter reconnaissance  Lightning caused fire single strike in timber and ground fuels.  Aerial fire monitoring took place every 1-3 days until significant growth was determined to be in effect around July 29th, 2010.  The input of a ICS209 was determined due to the fire acreage showing 102.  August 9th, 2010 a WebCam was set in place to assist in the monitoring of the fire.  August 14th, 2010 the fire crossed onto USFS land, Sequoia National Forest.  A system of unified command was established between Sequoia & Kings Canyon National Parks and Sequoia National Forest.    Indirect fire line was established by crews on the East side of Lightning Creek to slow progression to the East.  This operation took place approximately August 20th, 2010.  This tactic was successful  Rain production in early October significantly slowed the growth of the fire.  Detailed version of incident operations is found in the originating dispatch office.  Additionally, the above total acreage includes acres burned on Sequoia & Kings Canyon National Parks (KNP) as well as Sequoia National Forest (SQF).

SEKI Park Ranger 6-1-3 reported this human caused fire on 8/6/16 at 1705. She stated that she had helped put out a fire in Lodgepole Campground on 8/5/16 around 1330. It was a 3 ft x 10 inch log that had been pitched down the hill. 6-1-3 plus another ranger doused the log and area with water and 6-1-3 took the log with her in her truck. I, Steff Willmon, performed followup with 613 by phone and also contacted DV-1 to ask if any of our units had patrolled this fire since it occurred. The Homers Nose 2 fire was ignited by lightening and reported by helicopter    H552 on Sept. 5, 2003.  Initial attack on the fire was difficult due to steep  and inaccessible terrain.  Direct attack was infeasible and unsafe.  Air       resources were extensively used to contain the east flank of the fire in       Squaw Creek.  Where possible, heli-repel crews and Type 1 handcrews were used  to construct helispots and contain the fire along the western flank.  Natural  barriers were used as much as possible.  Full containment/control was not      possible until the season ending event.  Fire was declared out on Nov. 17,     2003.                                                                           The fire was incorporated into the Kaweah-Kern Complex and command was event-  ually transferred to a Fire Use Management Team.  Additional fire information  is located in the WFSA and in the Kaweah-Kern fire package.

FireReport: The Sheep Creek fire was discovered by an NPS law enforecement ranger.  The fire was adjacent to a campground and started when an unidentified individual deliberately set a slash pile on fire.  Initial attack resources are summarized below:  ICT4 - Ben Jacobs Helicopter 552 Engine 51 Engine 52 Engine 41 Patrol 51 Crew 91 Horseshoe Meadow Hotshots  The fire was contained using direct attack with bucket support within two hours of discovery and mopped up and patrolled over the next 2-3 days.  The fire was burning in an area which was previously thinned earlier in the summer.  Suppression efforts were complicated by the presence of numerous slash piles which burned aggressively and contributed to short range spotting.  One campground was evacuated as a precaution.  NPS law enforcement personnel conducted several interviews with campers but were unable to determine the individual who started the fire.  There was one minor injury on the fire.  There was some confusion with the initial fire report due to the fact it was reported in the Sheep Creek drainage near to where the Roaring WFU was currently burning at a higher elevation.  Suggestions and lessons learned from this fire include:  - Reporting parties must specify clearly that a fire is a new start when burning in an area where a pre-existing WFU (or other fire) exists.  - Law enforcement individuals should contact Ash Mt. fire dispatch for reporting fires and not automatically go through normal park dispatch.  - The response plan should have been better followed.  There were too many points of resource ordering  - Park resources should use the code 3 response judiciously and with extreme caution on the narrow and winding park roads.  The fire was delcared out on 10/20/06 after receiving significant rain.
Electrical fire at Crystal Cave Kiosk spread into wildland fuels.  Suppression action taken until controlled.  Fire was patrolled until declared out.  BC91, Will Basye, was IC.

This was a lightning caused fire reported by SEKI backcountry park ranger 8-1-7 (Christina Gooch) who described a tree bursting into flames near the Tyndall Creek crossing. 8-1-7 called this fire into the park's 24 hour dispatch who then relayed the information to Ash Mountain Fire dispatch. The fire was initially reported being 6 to 8 feet from the tree with a low to moderate rate of spread. No structures or visitors were threatened at the time of the report. 8-1-7 stated the fire was possibly human caused. The location was 100 yards from Shepherd's Pass Trail and a single lodgepole pine was burning with approximately 12 feet of ground fuel burned. Upon arrival to the scene, 8-1-7 began constructing line and dumping buckets of water on the fire. The individual did not have fire protective clothing and gear and there was concern about her performing active suppression. At 1533 members of the SEKI helitack crew were placed into the fire to perform direct suppression. Once the fire was contained it was placed in patrol status. This fire was officially declared out by Sequoia Duty Officer Robert Sanders on 10/14/15 at 1200.

FireReport: This human caused fire was reported by Ranger 636 Brown as smoldering at the base of a Sequoia tree on the Big Trees trail in Giant Forest. It was reported as separate from a campfire that was reported earlier in the day on the same trail. It was reported to be 1/8th of a mile from the Alta Trail junction, burning in leaf litter. Engine 51 responded to perform containment and direct suppression on the fire. It was patrolled later and there was no smoke showing during that time. The fire was officially declared out by Acting FMO John Ziegler on 9/3/17 at 10:23. To our knowledge no formal investigation was performed.

H552 reported a smoke in the Slide Bluffs area of Kings Canyon National Park, on 08/13/05. WE had experienced a high volume of lightning strikes just prior to this. That coupled w/ the remote location of the fire made this wilderness fire a good candidate for a wildland fire used for resource benefit. The fire was burning with low intensity in fuel model 8 (red fir with a brush component). Since the fire was within the MMA of an already defined unit and was lightning caused, we chose to monitor the fire rather than to put it out. H552 checked on the fire weekly. It grew from .1 to .25 acres. The predominant activity was smoldering and at best it seemed that the fire had a potential to grow to 10-20 acres. On Oct. 16, 2005 the fire was declared out at .25 acres after a lengthy period of inactivity.

FireReport: On July 30, 2001 at approximately 1451 hrs., helicopter 552 reported a fire    located above the middle fork of the Kings River across from Slide Bluffs.     Initial size-up was 15 to 20 acres burning in timber and brush with active     flames visible.  The fire was backing down hill at a low rate of spread        with occassional torching observed.  Predominate fuel models were fuel         model 8 and 10, interspersed by small section of NFFL fuel model 4.  Weather   patterns were dry, but predicted stable throughout the course of the fire.     Stage I and Stage II assessments were completed and no threats to NPS or       other resources were identified.  The decision was made to manage the fire for resource benefits(Wildland Fire Use).  Athough the fire was not significantly  complex, because of the potential for large growth towards the parks western   boundary, a stage III analysis was completed on August 17.  This stage III     was updated to include several other small fires being managed for wildland    fire use and became the Burnt Complex on August 28, 2001.  FARSITE analysis    indicated that even under extreme weather conditions, the fire was unlikely    to reach the park or the MMA boundary.                                         The fire was monitored using both air and ground resources.  Initial ground    monitoring confirmed fire and fuels information beginning on July 31.  Ground  monitoring continued during all active phases of the fire.  Fire behavior      was predictable throughout the course of the fire, best described as flanking  spreads punctuated by short uphill runs.  Occassional torching was observed    however, most fire spread was through ground fuels.  The last significant      fire behavior consisted of a short, intense run through a manzanita stand in   mid-November.                                                                  The fire was extinguished by snowfall and declared out on December 17.

The following changes were made to this Fire Report as part of the conversion from SACS to WFMI on 05/14/2005. TYPE INIT ATTACK 4: U - Mapped to Y End of SACS to WFMI conversion notes.  A vehicle heading up to Giant Forest caught fire on a hairpin corner about 1   mile above Hospital Rock.  The four family members all got out of the ford     Explorer before it became fully involved.  The adjacent vegetation on both     sides of the road caught fire.                                                  John Ziegler, acting as ICT3, and Engine 72 responded direct from Ash Mtn. and were the first fire resources on scene.  E71, the structure engine w/ crew of  3, also responded immediately and worked to control and secure the vehicle     fire.  Do to extreme fire danger and location, the IC ordered the following    additional resources:  2 type 2 helicopters, 1 Air Attack, 1 Air Tanker, 4     medium engines, 4 handcrews, 1 water tender.  These resources closely          resembled those listed in the Sequoia and Kings Canyon Fire Response plan for  a high response.  By 1536, E4172 CDF, Bat. Chief 4112, E73 (from Santa Monica) and E51 were on scene.  Shortly after these resources arrived, Air Attack 15,  Helo 520 and 522 also arrived and were awaiting instructions.  At about the    time the helicopters arrived we had secured all sides of the fire and deter-   mined that there was no need for the additional aircraft or crews and thus     had all the additional resources cancelled.  The IC kept the water tender on   order.  At the beginning of the fire, the IC had the Generals Hwy. closed at   giant Fores and Hospital Rock.  The IC also instructed the law enforcement     rangers to do a sweep of the highway to make sure no visitors were on any      of the dangerous switchback above the fire.  The Morro Rock road was also      closed for a short period of time.  Vehicle fire and vegetation mop-up were    completed by 2000 and the highway was reopened.  Vehicle was loaded on a flat- bed and sent out of the park at 1838 hours.  The vehicle was a total loss.

FireReport: This was a wildland fire discoverd in the Upper Loop housing area in Grant Grove Village.  This fire had been reported at the same time 5612 Oscar had reported the Manzanita fire. Park Ridge Lookout did have a view of the fire and reported white smoke decreasing up and back down about a 1/4 mile from the maintenance area and to the east. The Kings District FMO responded to the fire and discovered it was behind his park residence. He described it as a 20 X 20 with light fuels and that powerlines had been involved and that the captain of Engine 51 was assisting. He asked for Arrowhead IHC to be contacted and to send a squad to assist with the fire. PG&E electric company was notified at the request of the Kings FMO to check the breaker close to the fire location. This fire is still under formal investigation and no further details have been provided on the cause to date. This fire was officially declared out by the Kings District FMO on 8/29/16 at 1700.
